title: Study on the robustness of the assessment procedure of the NWO Open Competition Domain Science-M
author: Blaakmeer, Merijn
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
An inter-committee experiment  
  Study on the robustness of the assessment procedure of the NWO Open Competition Domain Science-M  
 The NWO Domain Science has executed a study on the robustness of the assessment procedure in the funding scheme Open Competition Domain Science-M (in Dutch ‘Open Competitie ENW-M’; hereafter ‘ENW-M’). The main goal of this study was to learn to what extent the procedure for assessment by committees in this programme leads to a reproducible outcome.   The regular assessment procedure of the ENW-M round consists of three steps: 1) consultation of external referees, 2) rebuttal from applicants, 3) assessment by assessment committee. For this study, a controlled blind mock assessment was realised in which the third step was executed in duplicate. 72 proposals (from the call ENW-M, round 2022-2023) were assessed in three clusters by two committees each. The duplicate assessment has produced six different rankings in which the proposals are prioritised from best to poorest assessed proposal.  The outcomes of each duplicate assessment have been compared and analysed from various angles and with different methods to determine how reliable committees can rank proposals. The various methodologies indicate there is a certain degree of overlap between the rankings: the to-be-funded proposals (top 20%) and the bottom of the rankings (bottom 20%) are rather consistently identified. Nevertheless, there are also differences between the rankings, which mainly manifest themselves in the middle part of the ranking between the top 20% and bottom 20%. In many cases, the observed differences in the ranking can already be identified in the pre-rankings that are based on initial scores of the individual members before the committee convened.   In collaboration with researchers from TU Delft (Prof. Dr Geurt Jongbloed and team) a custom made statistical model has been developed based on historical data to determine the range of agreement between two rankings that can realistically be expected for duplicate assessments. The model shows that the observed outcomes are compliant with the expected range of agreement. Additionally, the model indicates that in all cases the rankings by the committees from the controlled blind mock assessment are aligned better than two random rankings.   Besides the statistical analysis, NWO Domain Science gathered qualitative input from the involved committee members during analysis meetings to explore the possible underlying causes for the differences in the rankings. It could be concluded that in many cases the differences are based on different interpretations by the committee members, for example regarding concepts like high-risk/high-gain, or a difference in the appreciation of the rebuttal. Only in exceptional cases does it seems that group dynamics within the committee, e.g. a dominant committee member in the discussion, have played a decisive role.   Based on the outcomes of the study, NWO deems the reproducibility of the committee’s assessment sufficiently high. According to NWO, the assessment procedure of ENW-M can be considered as just and the study does not give rise to large alterations of NWO’s assessment procedure for the ENW-M programme. However, NWO acknowledges that the procedure can be further optimised. NWO recognises that the panel-based assessment procedure can come with difficult decisions that appear to be fully objective, while, in fact, there may be some degree of randomness. NWO will explore how to further optimise the assessment criteria and instructions to committee members with ENW-M, including the potential introduction of a scoring rubric as a measure to reduce matters related to interpretation and definitions of the criteria.
